content.full_text:
FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES COMMISSION
428 J Street • Suite 620 • Sacramento, CA 95814-2329
(916) 322-5660 • Fax (916) 322-0886

January 27, 2011


Randy Dickey
Walnut Creek Police Association
1630 N. Main St. #451
Walnut Creek, CA 94596

Re: Your Request for Advice
Our File No. A-10-173

Dear Mr. Dickey:

This letter is in response to your request for advice on behalf of the Walnut Creek Police Association PAC regarding the campaign reporting provisions of the Political Reform Act (the "Act").¹ While the Commission does not generally give advice involving past conduct, we do give advice involving campaign filing obligations based on past campaign activity. This letter is based on the facts as they have been presented to us; the Commission does not act as the finder of fact in providing advice. (In re Oglesby (1975) 1 FPPC Ops. 71.) Please bear in mind that nothing in this letter should be construed as evaluation of any conduct which may already have taken place.

QUESTION

Is the Walnut Creek Police Officer's Association (POA) considered a "Major Donor" and therefore required to file an FPPC Major Donor Campaign Statement (Form 461), and, if POA is a Major Donor Campaign Statement, when should it be filed?

CONCLUSION

Yes, POA is a major donor committee and is required to file a Major Donor Campaign Report (Form 461), which should have been filed by October 5, 2010, reporting the $28,000 contribution made on September 21, 2010.

________________________

¹ The Political Reform Act is contained in Government Code Sections 81000 through 91014. All statutory references are to the Government Code, unless otherwise indicated. The regulations of the Fair Political Practices Commission are contained in Sections 18110 through 18997 of Title 2 of the California Code of Regulations. All regulatory references are to Title 2, Division 6 of the California Code of Regulations, unless otherwise indicated.

File No. A-10-173
Page No. 2

FACTS

The Walnut Creek Police Association PAC (WCPA PAC) was formed as a campaign
committee in August of 2010, and assigned FPPC #1330754. The WCPA PAC is sponsored by
POA and by the Walnut Creek Police Management Association (PMA). According to the
Secretary of State (SOS) website and confirmed by you by telephone on November 10, 2010, the
WCPA PAC is a city general purpose recipient committee.

POA is a 501(c)(4) organization formed to represent the Walnut Creek Police Officers in
labor negotiations and contracts. It also provides Walnut Creek Police Officers with legal
insurance and sponsors a widows and orphans fund.

On September 21, 2010, POA made a $28,000 contribution to the WCPA PAC. While
your letter noted that this contribution was the first political activity of POA, you clarified in
your letter and in a telephone conversation with me that POA had made a few small donations in
past years, but has never made a total of $1,000 or more in donations during any calendar year prior to
making this $28,000 donation. You also indicated during our telephone discussion that, while
POA has not yet made any other donations for political purposes this year, they may begin
funding the WCPA PAC with regular dues payments in the future. You were not sure, but
thought it was likely that this funding would not begin until 2011.

In your letter you also noted that the source of the monies contributed to WCPA PAC
came from POA's savings account, which is made up primarily of member dues. In our
subsequent telephone conversation on November 19, 2010, you clarified that the contribution
was made up of savings account monies and did not include any contributions that were donated
to POA specifically for political purposes, but that it did include membership dues and some
fundraising proceeds. You estimated that the fundraising proceeds were less than $1,000 of the
$28,000 donation.

On January 12, 2011, you stated in our telephone conversation that the 460 filed by
WCPA PAC reported the contributions made by POA, and that the report was signed by the
committee treasurer, Kindee Durkee, and was not signed by a responsible officer of POA.

ANALYSIS

The Act provides that entities participating in California elections by making donations to
political campaigns may be required to file periodic campaign reports when those campaign
contributions meet certain thresholds (Section 84100, et seq.). The type and timing of those
reports depends upon (1) whether the donations are "contributions" under the Act; (2) whether
the entity making the contribution(s) qualifies as a committee; (3) if the entity is a committee, the
type of committee; and (4) the amount and timing of the contribution(s).

File No. A-10-173
Page No. 3

Contribution

To determine whether an entity has a filing obligation, it must first be determined if the
entity made a "contribution." The Act defines a "contribution" under Section 82015, in part, as
follows:

(a) "Contribution" means a payment, a forgiveness of a loan, a payment of a loan
by a third party, or an enforceable promise to make a payment except to the
extent that full and adequate consideration is received, unless it is clear from
the surrounding circumstances that it is not made for political purposes."

POA's $28,000 payment meets the definition of "contribution" because it was made for
political purposes. Therefore, POA has made a "contribution" under the Act.

Committee

The next step in determining whether POA has a filing obligation is to determine whether
the contributions it makes or receives would qualify POA as a "committee" under the Act.

The Act defines three different types of committees under Section 82013:

"Committee" means any person or combination of persons who directly or indirectly does
any of the following:

"(a) Receives contributions totaling one thousand dollars ($1,000) or more in a
calendar year;
"(b) Makes independent expenditures totaling one thousand dollars ($1,000) or
more in a calendar year; or
"(c) Makes contributions totaling ten thousand dollars ($10,000) or more in a
calendar year to or at the behest of candidates or committees."

In order to determine whether POA qualifies as a recipient committee under 82013(a), it
must be established that POA has received qualifying contributions under the Act.

Organizations like POA are commonly referred to as "multi-purpose" organizations; they
are established for purposes other than making contributions or independent expenditures, but
will, at times, make political expenditures. Nonprofit organizations which have a purpose that is
for other than making political donations are common examples. Since POA was formed to
represent Walnut Creek Police Officers in labor negotiations and contracts as well as to provide
legal insurance for the police officers, it would be considered a multi-purpose organization.
Because these organizations do not generally segregate funds received and used for political
purposes from those for non-political purposes, the Commission adopted FPPC Regulation
18215(b)(1), which sets out a rule for those organizations to use for determining when a payment
received by the multi-purpose organization is considered a "contribution" under the Act.

File No. A-10-173
Page No. 4

The regulation states, in part:

"Any payment made to a person or organization other than a candidate or committee, when, at the time of making the payment, the donor knows or has reason to know that the payment, or funds with which the payment will be commingled, will be used to make contributions or expenditures. If the donor knows or has reason to know that only part of the payment will be used to make contributions or expenditures, the payment shall be apportioned on a reasonable basis in order to determine the amount of the contribution.

"There shall be a presumption that the donor does not have reason to know that all or part of the payment will be used to make expenditures or contributions, unless the person or organization has made expenditures or contributions of at least one thousand dollars ($1,000) in the aggregate during the calendar year in which the payment occurs, or any of the immediately preceding four calendar years."

This provision in the regulation is commonly referred to as the "one bite of the apple" rule. A multi-purpose organization is receiving "contributions" if its members or donors make payments to the organization (including dues) and, at the time of making the payment, the donor knows or has reason to know that the funds with which the payment will be commingled will be used to make contributions or expenditures. There is a presumption that the donor does not have reason to know that all or part of the payment made to the multipurpose organization will be used for political activities in California unless the organization has made expenditures or contributions of at least $1,000 in the aggregate during the current calendar year or any of the immediately preceding four calendar years.

Since POA's $28,000 contribution made on September 21, 2010 is the only contribution of $1,000 or more that POA has made during any calendar year, and since POA has not received any contributions specifically for political purposes, it has not yet "received" contributions which would qualify it as a recipient committee. Therefore it has not yet qualified as a recipient committee under Section 82013(a). POA has, however, taken its "first bite of the apple" and would qualify as a recipient committee if it made additional contributions totaling $1,000 or more during 2010 or in the next four calendar years.

POA has not made any independent expenditures, so does not qualify as an independent expenditure committee under 82013(b).

However, since POA made a contribution over $10,000 during 2010, it qualified as a major donor committee under Section 82013(c) when it made a contribution of $28,000 to the WCPA PAC. Since POA's only contribution was made to WCPA PAC, a city general purpose committee under Section 82027.5(d) and FPPC Regulation 18247.5(b)(3).

File No. A-10-173
Page No. 5

Duty to File

City major donor committees are required to file:

• Semi-annual reports each year no later than July 31 for the period ending June 30, and no
later than January 31 for the period ending December 31, if the committee made
contributions or independent expenditures, including payments to a slate mailer
organization, during the six-month period before the closing date of the statements
(Section 84200(b)).
• Pre-election reports by October 5 for the period ending September 30, and 12 days before
the election for the period ending 17 days before the election, if contributions or
independent expenditures totaling $500 or more are made during the period covered by
the statement (Sections 84200.5(g)(1) and 84200.7(b).
• Late contribution reports within 24 hours of making a contribution of $1,000 or more or
making an independent expenditure of $1,000 or more in connection with a candidate or
measure being voted on in that election (Section 84203 and 84204) during the 16 days
prior to an election.

Thus, as a city major donor committee, POA was required to file a major donor pre-
election campaign statement (Form 461) reporting the $28,000 contribution it made on
September 21, 2010 on a pre-election campaign report for the period ending September 30, 2010.
This report should have been filed by October 5, 20102.

Alternatively, if POA, as a sponsoring committee of WCPA PAC, met all of the
following criteria, it would not qualify as a committee, and it would not be required to file
committee reports pursuant to FPPC Regulation 18419:

• The sponsor does not directly or indirectly make or receive $1,000 or more in
contributions during a calendar year, other than in support of its sponsored committee.
• The sponsored committee reports all contributions and expenditures made in support of
the committee by the sponsor, its intermediate units, and the members of such entities.
• The sponsored committee reports the sponsor as an intermediary if the sponsor provides
the committee with $100 or more in contributions from members, regardless of whether
any member contributes $100 or more.
• A responsible officer of the sponsor, as well as the treasurer of the sponsored committee,
verifies the committee's campaign statements.

Since a responsible officer from POA did not sign WCPA PAC's Form 460, the Form
461 was required. However, you may wish to utilize this alternate reporting method in the future
if your committee meets all of the above mentioned criteria.

2 Upon receipt of your letter dated October 9, 2010, our office ascertained that the Walnut Creek Police
Officer's Association (POA) had filing obligations. In order to provide for a timelier filing, FPPC staff spoke with
you on October 27, 2010, and advised you to file the major donor report, Form 461 with the Walnut Creek City
Clerk's Office. On January 12, 2011, you confirmed to me that the 461 had been filed.

File No. A-10-173
Page No. 6

Lastly, please be advised that this letter addresses POA's filing obligations under the
Political Reform Act. The City of Walnut Creek may impose additional reporting or other
requirements. This is because cities and counties may enact ordinances that impose additional or
different disclosure requirements. We encourage you to check with the Walnut Creek City
Clerk's office to determine whether local ordinances require additional reporting or other
requirements.

If you have any other questions regarding this matter, please contact me at
(916) 322-5660.

Sincerely,

Scott Hallabrin
General Counsel

[Sandy Johnson]
By: Sandy Johnson
Political Reform Consultant
